one_line_takeaway: FedShare adds personalized, revocable data-sharing to federated recommenders via a dual contrastive learning/unlearning scheme that beats prior gradient-history-based unlearning baselines (FRU, CUFRU) in both accuracy and storage cost across three datasets -- but this is a recommender-systems/federated-learning privacy-compliance paper (GDPR-motivated), not an adversarial AI-security paper: no attacker or attack is modeled or evaluated.
key_finding: Learning phase (Table III, HR@20/NDCG@20): FedShare achieves the best scores on all three datasets among personalized-sharing methods -- MovieLens-1M 0.4773/0.0659 vs. CDCGNNFed 0.4685/0.0651, PDC-FRS 0.4662/0.0647, FedShare-FedAvg 0.4656/0.0642; Fashion 0.0607/0.0074 vs. CDCGNNFed 0.0588/0.0069, PDC-FRS 0.0569/0.0057, FedShare-FedAvg 0.0564/0.0053; Video Game 0.2614/0.0198 vs. CDCGNNFed 0.2532/0.0185, PDC-FRS 0.2576/0.0176, FedShare-FedAvg 0.2533/0.0174. Unlearning phase: FedShare (0.4665/0.0647 on ML-1M, 0.0549/0.0056 on Fashion, 0.2526/0.0172 on Video Game) beats FRU and CUFRU on all three datasets/metrics but does not reach the Retrain oracle (0.4682/0.0651, 0.0552/0.0063, 0.2534/0.0191 respectively) -- the paper explicitly frames this gap as expected since FedShare avoids full retraining. Fig. 4 (bar charts, no exact values in extracted table) shows FedShare has competitive unlearning time (much faster than Retrain, comparable to FRU/CUFRU) and lower storage overhead than FRU and CUFRU across all three datasets, attributed to storing only a handful of historical item-embedding snapshots rather than per-client gradient histories. Ablation (Table IV) confirms each component matters: removing contrastive learning (w/o CL) drops ML-1M HR@20 from 0.4773 to 0.4702; removing BPR (w/o BPR) drops it to 0.4725; removing the forgotten graph in unlearning drops HR@20 from 0.4665 to 0.4624; skipping remaining-data federated retraining (w/o remaining FL) drops it to 0.4642.
core_contribution: The paper proposes FedShare, a federated recommender-system framework that unifies (1) fully personalized user data sharing -- each user chooses to fully share, partially share, or withhold their interaction data with the server -- with (2) support for later 'data unsharing'/unlearning requests that remove previously shared interactions and their influence on the trained model. In the learning phase, the server builds a high-order user-item bipartite graph from shared interactions and uses LightGCN-style propagation plus an InfoNCE contrastive-learning loss to align a 'local view' (FedAvg-aggregated client embeddings) with a 'global view' (graph-propagated embeddings), on top of a standard BPR recommendation loss. In the unlearning phase, the paper introduces a 'contrastive unlearning' mechanism: after removing unshared edges from the server graph, new local views (from federated retraining on remaining data) are pulled toward new global views (from the updated graph) and pushed away from 'forgotten views' -- item embeddings reconstructed by running graph propagation on a small number (M) of stored historical global embedding snapshots over a 'forgotten graph' built from the unshared interactions. This avoids storing large volumes of historical per-client gradients, which is what prior federated-recommendation-unlearning methods (FRU, CUFRU) require.
threat_model: None. This is not an adversarial-security paper: there is no attacker, no adversarial capability assumption, and no attack/defense evaluation. The only 'threat' discussed is a regulatory/privacy-compliance one -- GDPR's 'Right to be Forgotten' -- motivating why a user's previously shared data and its trained-model influence should be removable on request. The paper does not claim or evaluate the unlearning mechanism against any information-leakage, membership-inference, or gradient-inversion attack; it only evaluates whether the unlearned model's recommendation accuracy is close to a retrain-from-scratch oracle and how much storage/time it costs.
limitations: The authors themselves state two limitations: (1) the unlearning phase still requires participation/retraining from the remaining clients, adding client-side communication and computation overhead, and the authors propose exploring fully server-side unlearning as future work; (2) the framework only supports unlearning for users who previously shared data -- it does not support unlearning/removal requests from users who never shared data but only participated in ordinary federated training. Additionally (from the paper's own results, not stated as a limitation): FedShare's unlearned model never matches the Retrain oracle's accuracy, meaning the 'approximate unlearning' guarantee is empirical/utility-based (Θ̃s ≈ Θ*_s) rather than certified/provable removal of influence -- no formal unlearning guarantee (e.g., certified removal bound) is given or evaluated.

datasets_used: Video Game (Amazon reviews) (15,517 users, 37,077 items, 284,867 interactions)
quantitative_results: Learning phase HR@20/NDCG@20 -- MovieLens-1M: FedShare 0.4773/0.0659, CDCGNNFed 0.4685/0.0651, PDC-FRS 0.4662/0.0647, FedShare-FedAvg 0.4656/0.0642
Learning phase HR@20/NDCG@20 -- Fashion: FedShare 0.0607/0.0074, CDCGNNFed 0.0588/0.0069, PDC-FRS 0.0569/0.0057, FedShare-FedAvg 0.0564/0.0053
Learning phase HR@20/NDCG@20 -- Video Game: FedShare 0.2614/0.0198, CDCGNNFed 0.2532/0.0185, PDC-FRS 0.2576/0.0176, FedShare-FedAvg 0.2533/0.0174
Unlearning phase HR@20/NDCG@20 -- MovieLens-1M: Retrain 0.4682/0.0651, FedShare 0.4665/0.0647, CUFRU 0.4634/0.0633, FRU 0.4632/0.0634
Unlearning phase HR@20/NDCG@20 -- Fashion: Retrain 0.0552/0.0063, FedShare 0.0549/0.0056, FRU 0.0531/0.0046, CUFRU 0.0526/0.0048
Unlearning phase HR@20/NDCG@20 -- Video Game: Retrain 0.2534/0.0191, FedShare 0.2526/0.0172, FRU 0.2522/0.0163, CUFRU 0.2515/0.0159

Ablation on MovieLens-1M HR@20/NDCG@20: full FedShare (learning) 0.4773/0.0659, w/o CL 0.4702/0.0634, w/o BPR 0.4725/0.0638; full FedShare (unlearning) 0.4665/0.0647, w/o forgotten graph 0.4624/0.0613, w/o remaining FL 0.4642/0.0635

Fig. 4 reports unlearning time and storage overhead as bar charts only (no exact numeric values extracted); qualitatively FedShare < FRU, CUFRU in storage overhead on all 3 datasets, and far faster than Retrain in unlearning time
baselines_compared: FedShare-FedAvg (naive server-as-special-client FedAvg baseline, learning phase)
CDCGNNFed (authors' prior work, ref [12], learning phase)
PDC-FRS (differential-privacy data contribution, ref [14], learning phase)
FRU (client-side historical-gradient federated unlearning, ref [15], unlearning phase)
CUFRU (server-side gradient-transfer-station unlearning, ref [16], unlearning phase)
